summary: English Pre-Raphaelite painter. Known for Work (laborers with Thomas Carlyle observing, centermost figure borrows Apollo Belvedere pose, took 13 years), The Last of England (tondo of emigrant couple under umbrella before Dover, inspired by Woolner), and Manchester Murals for Alfred Waterhouse's Manchester Town Hall. Designed stained glass including King Rene's Honeymoon. Grandson Ford Madox Ford wrote The Good Soldier.

works.name: Work (1852–1865)
works.description: Laborers (navvies) replacing a roadway; Thomas Carlyle and Frederick Maurice observe from the right. Centermost figure borrows pose from Apollo Belvedere. Background includes campaigners with "Vote for Bobus" sandwich boards. Took 13 years to complete. Housed in Manchester Art Gallery.

works.name: The Last of England (1855)
works.description: A tondo (circular painting) of an emigrant couple turning away from England. Wife holds hands of both husband and otherwise-invisible baby while sheltering under a black umbrella. Paddle-steamer visible before White Cliffs of Dover. Inspired by sculptor Thomas Woolner's emigration to Australia. Held at the Fitzwilliam Museum, Cambridge.

recursive_suggestions: Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood — Brown was closely associated though not a formal member
Thomas Carlyle — appears in Work and his writings influenced the movement
Dante Gabriel Rossetti — fellow Pre-Raphaelite, collaborated with Brown
comprehensive_summary: Ford Madox Brown (1821–1893) was an English painter closely associated with the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood, alongside Dante Gabriel Rossetti and William Holman Hunt, though he was never a formal member. His most famous painting, Work (1852–1865), took thirteen years to complete and depicts navvies digging up a road while Thomas Carlyle and Frederick Maurice observe from the right. The centermost laborer borrows his pose from the Apollo Belvedere. The background includes 'Vote for Bobus' sandwich boards (Bobus being a corrupt politician from Carlyle's writings), and the foreground features a girl in a garnet dress holding a baby. His other major painting, The Last of England (1855), is a tondo (circular painting) showing an emigrant couple turning away from the White Cliffs of Dover under a black umbrella. Brown painted it when he was 'very hard up and a little mad' and considering emigrating himself; it was inspired by sculptor Thomas Woolner's departure for Australia. The painting is held at the Fitzwilliam Museum in Cambridge. Brown also created twelve murals for the Manchester Town Hall (designed by Alfred Waterhouse, the tallest town hall in the UK) and designed stained glass including a piece depicting King Rene's Honeymoon. His grandson was the novelist Ford Madox Ford, author of The Good Soldier.
